en.extract:
The Madagascar flufftail (Sarothrura insularis) is a species of bird in the family Sarothruridae.
It is endemic to Madagascar. The subspecies is Monotypic.
Madagascar flufftails are found in Madagascar however, they are limited in the wet areas in the north and lacking from Lake Sahaka in the northeast.
Its natural habitat is subtropical, tropical moist lowland forests, shrubland, grassland, and artificial areas. This is where they find their food which consists of insects and seeds.
Madagascar Flufftails have a stable population of around 6,700-67,000. The species does not meet the criteria for vulnerability, therefore, it is marked as Least Concern.


Characteristics
The Madagascar flufftail is small in size with a length of around 14cm and a weight of 30g.
The head, neck, and breast of the adult male are chestnut rufous while the side of the head, the chin, and the throat are paler. Their back and stomach are black with stripes of white/yellow. The immature male is similar to the adult male except has a more dull color.
The females have a dull color. They have dark upper parts with chestnut streaks. The upper tail is barred cinnamon-buff and the longest upper tail is deep chestnut with a hint of black. The immature female is similar to the adult except for spotting on the underparts and duller color.


Sounds
The Madagascar flufftail's call does not differ by sex. The most common call is a high-pitched, loud, "kee" or "keekee" sound that lasts around 20 seconds. A less common call includes a "drr-drr" and ends with a "kik". 
In copulation, the mates give each other an "ii…ii…ii" sound. 
While nesting the male gives the female a "tee-chh, tee-chh" and she responds with "chh-chh"


Reproduction
The Madagascar Flufftail engages in its nesting activities at different times of the year depending on the geographical region. In the northeastern (NE) part of its habitat, nesting typically takes place in February, coinciding with the local wet season from November to April. Conversely, in the southern part of Madagascar, nesting is observed in October, aligning with the local wet season there. There is also a likelihood of nesting occurring in September and possibly even in July.
This avian species is known for its distinctive nesting behavior. Unlike many, the Madagascar Flufftail opts to create a unique domed nest. These nests can be found on the ground, or elevated at heights of up to 160 centimeters above the ground.


Habitat
This species is known for its capacity to live in different habitats. It has a preference for grasslands, frequently inhabiting the edges and clearings of both undisturbed and degraded forest areas. Additionally, it readily adapts to secondary bush environments, characterized by the presence of large ferns and thick grasses. Although not documented in cassava fields, it has been observed in regions of intense cultivation. Moreover, this species can be encountered on the forest floor, highlighting its adaptability to various forest ecosystems. It is also known to flourish in marsh environments, often coexisting with tall grasses, reeds, sedges, and occasionally even rice paddies. Its habitat range spans from sea level to elevations as high as 2300 meters."

The '''Madagascar flufftail''' ('''''Sarothrura insularis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Sarothruridae]].
It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Madagascar]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite report |url=http://www.iucnredlist.org/details/22692241/0 |title=Sarothrura insularis: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2016: e.T22692241A93343786 |last=IUCN |date=2016-10-01 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2016-3.rlts.t22692241a93343786.en |access-date=12 November 2021 |language=en|doi-access=free }}</ref> The subspecies is Monotypic.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Taylor |first1=Barry |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |date=2020 |title=Madagascar Flufftail (Sarothrura insularis), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/madflu1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.madflu1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Madagascar flufftails are found in [[Madagascar]] however, they are limited in the wet areas in the north and lacking from Lake Sahaka in the northeast.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Madagascan Flufftail |url=https://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-madagascan-flufftail.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.oiseaux-birds.com}}</ref>

Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical, tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s,<ref name=":0" /> [[shrubland]], [[grassland]], and artificial areas.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |last=Red List |date=2023 |title=Madagascar Flufftail |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22692241/93343786}}</ref> This is where they find their food which consists of insects and seeds.<ref name=":1" />

Madagascar Flufftails have a stable [[Population (biology)|population]] of around 6,700-67,000. The species does not meet the criteria for [[vulnerability]], therefore, it is marked as [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Madagascar Flufftail (Sarothrura insularis) - BirdLife species factsheet |url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/22692241 |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref>

== Characteristics ==
The Madagascar flufftail is small in size with a [[length]] of around 14cm and a [[weight]] of 30g.

The [[head]], [[neck]], and [[breast]] of the adult [[male]] are chestnut rufous while the side of the head, the chin, and the throat are paler. Their back and stomach are black with stripes of white/yellow.<ref name=":2" /> The immature male is similar to the adult male except has a more dull color.<ref name=":1" />

The [[Female|females]] have a dull color. They have dark upper parts with chestnut streaks. The upper tail is barred cinnamon-buff and the longest upper tail is deep chestnut with a hint of black.<ref name=":2" /> The immature female is similar to the adult except for spotting on the underparts and duller color.<ref name=":1" />

== Sounds ==
The Madagascar flufftail's call does not differ by sex. The most common call is a high-pitched, loud, "kee" or "keekee" sound that lasts around 20 seconds. A less common call includes a "drr-drr" and ends with a "kik". 

In [[copulation (zoology)|copulation]], the mates give each other an "ii…ii…ii" sound. 

While [[Nesting instinct|nesting]] the male gives the female a "tee-chh, tee-chh" and she responds with "chh-chh"<ref>{{Cite web |title=Madagascar Flufftail (Sarothrura insularis) :: xeno-canto |url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Sarothrura-insularis |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=xeno-canto.org}}</ref><ref name=":2" />

== Reproduction ==
The Madagascar Flufftail engages in its [[Nesting instinct|nesting]] activities at different times of the year depending on the [[geographical region]]. In the northeastern (NE) part of its [[habitat]], nesting typically takes place in February, coinciding with the local [[wet season]] from November to April. Conversely, in the southern part of Madagascar, nesting is observed in October, aligning with the local wet season there. There is also a likelihood of nesting occurring in September and possibly even in July.

This avian species is known for its distinctive nesting behavior. Unlike many, the Madagascar Flufftail opts to create a unique domed nest. These nests can be found on the ground, or elevated at heights of up to 160 centimeters above the ground.<ref name=":3" />

== Habitat ==
This species is known for its capacity to live in different habitats. It has a preference for [[Grassland|grasslands]], frequently inhabiting the edges and clearings of both undisturbed and [[degraded forest]] areas.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, it readily [[Adaptation|adapts]] to secondary bush environments, characterized by the presence of large [[Fern|ferns]] and thick grasses.<ref name=":2" /> Although not documented in [[cassava]] fields, it has been observed in regions of intense [[Cultivation (agriculture)|cultivation]]. Moreover, this species can be encountered on the [[forest]] floor, highlighting its adaptability to various forest ecosystems.<ref name=":1" /> It is also known to flourish in [[marsh]] environments, often [[coexisting]] with tall grasses, reeds, sedges, and occasionally even rice paddies. Its habitat range spans from sea level to [[Elevation|elevations]] as high as 2300 meters."<ref name=":2" />
